topic.topic: When should an agent discover a new skill rather than compose existing skills?

topic_summary: agent_lab research package for: When should an agent discover a new skill rather than compose existing skills?

primitive_definitions.name: Skill
primitive_definitions.definition: A reusable action primitive, parameterized by context, that the agent can invoke to perform a specific subtask (e.g., picking up an object, clicking a button).
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Skills are the atomic units of agent behavior; they cannot be further decomposed without losing generality.

primitive_definitions.name: Composition
primitive_definitions.definition: The process of sequentially combining multiple existing skills to achieve a complex task, typically via planning or prompting.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Composition is the baseline mechanism for leveraging prior knowledge without acquiring new abilities.

primitive_definitions.name: Discovery
primitive_definitions.definition: The process of generating a new skill (e.g., via reinforcement learning or fine-tuning) for a subtask that cannot be effectively performed by composing existing skills.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Discovery is the fundamental mechanism for expanding the agent's repertoire beyond given skills.

assumptions.assumption: Composition failure can be reliably detected via a performance metric (e.g., success rate below a threshold after a fixed number of attempts).
assumptions.rationale: Without a reliable detection signal, it is impossible to trigger discovery only when needed.
assumptions.possible_failure: The metric may be noisy or task-specific, leading to false positives (unnecessary discovery) or false negatives (missed opportunities for discovery).

assumptions.assumption: The cost of discovery (time, compute, sample efficiency) is justified only when composition is insufficient for the task at hand.
assumptions.rationale: If composition works well, discovery wastes resources and may produce redundant skills; thus a trade-off exists.
assumptions.possible_failure: Discovery may be beneficial even when composition succeeds, e.g., to create more efficient or generalizable skills, invalidating the assumption.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: The agent uses a threshold-based decision rule: after a fixed number of composition attempts (k=3), if the success rate across attempts falls below a tuned threshold τ (e.g., 0.5), it triggers skill discovery; otherwise, it continues composing. The threshold is tuned on a validation set of tasks that are separate from the test tasks.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

The threshold is tuned on a validation set of tasks that are separate from the test tasks.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: The agent uses a threshold-based decision rule: after a fixed number of composition attempts (k=3), if the success rate across attempts falls below a tuned threshold τ (e.g., 0.5), it triggers skill discovery; otherwise, it continues composing. The threshold is tuned on a validation set of tasks that are separate from the test tasks.

tensions.statement: The tension between exploiting existing skills (composition) and exploring new ones (discovery) in resource-constrained environments: composition is efficient but may fail on novel subtasks, while discovery is costly but can overcome such failures.
tensions.why_it_matters: This work provides a principled, tunable decision rule for skill discovery vs composition, going beyond ad-hoc approaches in existing LLM agents. It also establishes a benchmark for controlled novelty evaluation, differentiating between composable and novel subtasks.

candidate_questions.question: Under what conditions should an LLM agent trigger skill discovery instead of composing existing skills?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: This work provides a principled, tunable decision rule for skill discovery vs composition, going beyond ad-hoc approaches in existing LLM agents. It also establishes a benchmark for controlled novelty evaluation, differentiating between composable and novel subtasks.

tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: This work provides a principled, tunable decision rule for skill discovery vs composition, going beyond ad-hoc approaches in existing LLM agents. research_question.question: Under what conditions should an LLM agent trigger skill discovery instead of composing existing skills?
research_question.expected_contribution: This work provides a principled, tunable decision rule for skill discovery vs composition, going beyond ad-hoc approaches in existing LLM agents. It also establishes a benchmark for controlled novelty evaluation, differentiating between composable and novel subtasks.

hypothesis.statement: An agent that adaptively triggers skill discovery when composition success rate falls below a tuned threshold will achieve higher overall task completion and efficiency compared to always composing or always discovering, when evaluated on a mixture of composable and novel tasks.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: The adaptive agent will outperform both baselines in terms of success rate and total reward, especially on tasks containing both in-distribution (composable) and out-of-distribution (novel) subtasks.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: Compare three agents: (1) Always compose (prompt-based, no new skills), (2) Always discover (attempts to create a new skill for every subtask via reinforcement learning from scratch), (3) Adaptive agent using threshold rule. Evaluate on ALFWorld and WebShop tasks modified to include both composable sub-tasks (in-distribution) and novel sub-tasks (out-of-distribution). For each condition, run 10 seeds. Measure success rate, average steps, and cumulative reward. Perform two-sample t-tests (α=0.05) to compare adaptive vs baselines. Tune threshold τ over values {0.3, 0.5, 0.7} on a validation set of tasks not used in test; report results for best τ.
independent_variables: Decision rule: always compose, always discover, adaptive threshold
controls: Same set of seed skills for composition
Same maximum number of attempts per task (k=3)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: The adaptive agent does not significantly outperform the always-compose baseline on novel tasks; or it performs worse than the always-discover baseline on tasks requiring many new skills.

supports_hypothesis: Adaptive agent achieves significantly higher success rate and reward than always-compose on novel tasks.
Adaptive agent requires fewer steps than always-discover on tasks with few novel subtasks.
rejects_hypothesis: Adaptive agent matches always-compose but fails to improve on novel tasks.
Adaptive agent triggers discovery too often (wasting resources) or too rarely (missing opportunities), resulting in performance similar to one of the baselines.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If the adaptive rule fails significantly, alternative decision mechanisms (e.g., meta-learning a trigger based on task embeddings, or using a cascade of uncertainty estimates) will be explored.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Composition failure can be reliably detected via a performance metric (e.g., success rate below a threshold after a fixed number of attempts).
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Under what conditions should an LLM agent trigger skill discovery instead of composing existing skills?

review.summary: Baseline output converted to the common FirstResearch evaluation schema.
strengths: Generated by the same LLM backend as FirstResearch
weaknesses: Not constrained by the full FirstResearch staged derivation process

related_work_claims: Composing existing skills via prompting or few-shot learning works well for tasks that are decomposable into known subroutines, but fails for novel tasks requiring new sub-skills.
Skill discovery methods like self-play or learning from scratch are sample-inefficient and may lead to redundant skills; thus, agents should only discover when composition is insufficient.
literature_plan.inferred_gap: Current LLM agents lack a principled decision rule to determine when to trigger skill discovery versus composition, leading to either wasteful exploration or insufficient generalization.
closest_baselines: ReAct (Yao et al., 2022): Composes reasoning and action via prompting, but does not discover new skills.
Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023): Learns which tools (skills) to call, but assumes tool set is fixed.
SayCan (Ahn et al., 2022): Composes skills for robots but uses predefined skills with affordances.

experiment_plan.proposed_question: Under what conditions should an LLM agent trigger skill discovery instead of composing existing skills?
experiment_plan.proposed_hypothesis: An agent should trigger skill discovery when the cumulative reward from compositional attempts on novel tasks falls below a threshold, indicating composition is insufficient, balancing exploration and exploitation.
experiment_plan.experiment_design: Compare three agents: (1) Always compose (ReAct-like), (2) Always discover (learn from scratch per task), (3) Adaptive agent that decides based on a performance threshold: if composition fails to achieve a minimal reward after k attempts, it triggers discovery. Use a set of benchmark tasks (e.g., ALFWorld, WebShop) modified to include both composable and novel sub-tasks. Measure success rate, number of steps, and total reward. Run 5 seeds per condition.
implementation_risks: Defining a reliable 'composition failure' metric may be task-specific.
Novelty detection may require additional overhead or pre-training.
Computational cost of discovery steps could be high for complex skills.
Threshold hyperparameter sensitivity across task distributions.

strengths: Addresses a relevant and under-explored question in LLM agents: when to trigger skill discovery versus composition.
Clear hypothesis and experimental design with three contrasting conditions (always compose, always discover, adaptive).
Uses established benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop) that are standard for evaluating LLM agents.
Acknowledges implementation risks and potential sensitivity of the threshold hyperparameter.
weaknesses: The threshold-based decision rule is simplistic and lacks theoretical justification or empirical basis.
Operational definition of 'novelty' for tasks is missing; benchmarks may not cleanly separate composable vs. novel subtasks.
Baselines are somewhat outdated; missing recent skill discovery methods like Voyager or generative agents.
'Always discover' baseline is unrealistic (learning from scratch per task) and may not be a fair comparison.
Small sample size (5 seeds) with no plan for statistical significance testing.
Critical threshold hyperparameter is not justified; no plan for tuning or validation across diverse tasks.
required_revisions: Provide an operational definition of task novelty for the benchmarks (e.g., out-of-distribution subtasks).
Include more recent baselines such as Voyager, CodeAct, or AdaPlanner that also address skill composition/discovery.
Justify the adaptive threshold rule with theoretical reasoning or preliminary experiments; consider alternative decision mechanisms.
Develop a plan for tuning the threshold (e.g., using validation tasks or meta-learning).
Increase number of seeds (≥10) and specify statistical tests (e.g., t-tests, confidence intervals).
Concretely define the 'composition failure metric' (e.g., success rate below threshold after k attempts).
Avoid overclaiming: revise hypothesis to state that the rule is a candidate decision mechanism to be evaluated.
